worth_it_explanation: Floor strategy sets the price below which an impression goes unsold, so it controls the trade between fill rate and the value of each impression a publisher sells through OpenX. A floor that leans entirely on a dynamic model, or one flat number applied across every placement, format, country, and device, can leave revenue on the table without anyone noticing. The check is a review of the floor rules a publisher already has in place across OpenX and its Prebid-based header bidding wrapper, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pricing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Floor pricing decides the minimum price a publisher will accept for an impression in the open auction, which sets where the line falls between filling more impressions and protecting the value of each one. OpenX is a sell-side platform (SSP) that runs Prebid-compatible header bidding, so a publisher's floors are carried both by OpenX's own auction and by the price floors configured in the Prebid wrapper. This subcategory is about the floor strategy itself, the values chosen and how they are applied, rather than the wrapper mechanics that carry them. These checks confirm that the floor strategy is a deliberate, evidence-based choice rather than automation accepted by default, and that the floors reflect the price differences across the publisher's inventory.

audit_checks.title: Floors are not segmented by inventory, format, geography, or device
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single flat floor across all inventory ignores that some placements, formats, countries, and devices clear at higher prices than others. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot serve inventory whose value varies across several dimensions at once.

educate.base:
A floor price works best when it matches what the impression is worth, and impressions are not worth the same across a publisher's inventory. An above-the-fold display unit on a high-engagement page clears higher than a unit far down a thin page. A video impression clears on a different scale from a standard banner. Traffic from a high-spending country clears higher than traffic from a market where advertiser demand is thin. Desktop, mobile web, and in-app inventory each carry their own demand and their own typical clearing price. A floor set as one flat number across all of this cannot fit inventory whose value varies along every one of those lines.

The cost of a flat floor cuts both ways. Set it to the average and it sits below the strong inventory, so impressions that could have cleared higher clear at the floor and the difference is left uncollected. Set it higher to protect the strong inventory and it sits above the weak inventory, so impressions that would have sold at a lower price go unsold instead. Either way, one number serves the middle and misprices the ends.

Because OpenX runs Prebid-compatible header bidding, a publisher sets floors in two places that need to agree. The OpenX auction applies the floors configured on its side, and the Prebid Price Floors Module applies floor rules in the wrapper. Prebid's floor rules can target dimensions that move clearing price, including ad unit, media type, ad size, and domain, and the list can be extended to other attributes of the page, user, or auction. That makes segmented floors a matter of writing rules per dimension rather than a single catch-all value.

A few signs point to floors that are not segmented:
  • One floor value covers all inventory, with no breakout by ad unit, format, geography, or device
  • Reporting shows average clearing prices that differ widely across placements, formats, countries, or devices while the floor stays the same for all of them
  • Premium placements clear at or near the same floor as filler inventory, which suggests the floor is not protecting their value
  • The OpenX-side floors and the Prebid wrapper floors do not line up, so one auction is pricing inventory the other is not
You may want to confirm that the floors reflect the price differences the inventory actually shows. One approach is to read average clearing price by ad unit, format, geography, and device, then set floor rules that follow those differences rather than a single number across the board.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a homepage or section-front placement usually clears higher than an article-footer unit, video clears higher than display, and audience from high-demand markets clears higher than audience from thin ones. You may want to set floors by placement, format, and geography so the premium inventory holds its value while the lower-value inventory keeps a floor low enough to stay filled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach an audience closer to a purchase and clear higher than editorial pages, and device matters because mobile shoppers and desktop shoppers carry different demand. You may want to segment floors so the commercial, higher-intent inventory carries a higher floor than the editorial inventory rather than sharing one number.

learn_more.source_title: Module - Price Floors for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for setting floors on the SSP side is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the floor values across ad units, formats, geographies, and device categories, on both the OpenX side and in the Prebid wrapper, to see whether they are differentiated or whether one flat floor covers everything.

openx_ui.label: OpenX publisher console
steps: Open the inventory or pricing section of the OpenX publisher console and list the active floor rules with their values.
For each rule, note which ad units, formats, geographies, and device categories it covers and at what floor.
Record whether the floors differ across those dimensions or whether a single floor applies across all inventory with no breakout.

prebid_config.label: Prebid wrapper configuration
steps: Inspect the Prebid Price Floors Module configuration in the page source or the wrapper management tool, and list the floor rules and the dimensions they target, for example ad unit, media type, ad size, and domain.
Compare the wrapper floors against the OpenX-side floors and note any inventory where the two do not agree.
Record whether the wrapper floors are segmented by dimension or whether a single catch-all value covers everything.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the floor values across ad units, formats, geographies, and device categories, on both the OpenX side and in the Prebid wrapper, alongside the average clearing price for each. Floors that follow the value differences the reporting shows, with higher floors on inventory that clears higher and lower floors on inventory that clears lower, and OpenX-side and wrapper floors that agree, pass. A single flat floor applied across inventory whose clearing prices differ widely, or OpenX-side and wrapper floors that disagree, is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the floor and the value diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor values and the average clearing price by ad unit, format, geography, and device above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies a single flat floor across inventory whose value differs by ad unit, format, geography, and device. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot fit inventory that clears at different prices. Because OpenX runs Prebid-compatible header bidding, the floors set on the OpenX side and the floor rules in the Prebid wrapper also need to agree. {{voice_recommend}} reading the average clearing price by ad unit, format, geography, and device, then setting floor rules that carry higher floors on the inventory that clears higher and lower floors on the inventory that clears lower, kept consistent across OpenX and the wrapper. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price across the segments to confirm the floors fit the value of each.

audit_checks.title: Dynamic floors run without a fixed-floor baseline to measure against
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A dynamic floor model adjusts the floor per auction toward yield, which often raises revenue, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, for example protecting a premium price point. Running dynamic floors without a fixed-floor baseline to compare against leaves the choice untested, so a publisher cannot tell whether the dynamic floor is helping or trading away inventory value on a slice of inventory.

educate.base:
A publisher selling through OpenX can set a floor as a fixed number it chooses and the auction applies as written, or hand the floor to a dynamic model that adjusts it per query to balance fill and value. In the Prebid wrapper, the Price Floors Module supports this directly. Floor values can be hard-coded into the ad unit, supplied as a static package, or loaded dynamically from a floor provider's endpoint and refreshed on its own schedule. The dynamic option often raises revenue, and it is built to find the price that fills the impression without giving the inventory away.

The risk is not that dynamic pricing is wrong. The risk is running it without a comparison. A dynamic floor optimizes toward the objective the model was built for, which is yield, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy. A publisher protecting a premium price point on a sponsorship-adjacent placement, or holding a deliberate floor to keep a direct-sold rate intact, may find the dynamic floor clears impressions below the price they meant to protect. Without a baseline to measure against, the publisher has no way to see that happening.

A fixed-floor baseline makes the dynamic floor measurable. A publisher can apply a dynamic floor to a slice of inventory, hold a fixed floor on a comparable slice, and read the revenue, fill rate, and average price across both. That turns the floor choice into a measured result rather than a default that was never questioned. The comparison matters more when a third-party floor provider supplies the dynamic values because the publisher is then trusting an outside model whose objective they did not set.

A few signs point to dynamic floors running without a baseline:
  • Floor values come entirely from a dynamic provider or model with no fixed-floor comparison on record
  • No experiment or before-and-after read exists showing how the dynamic floor performed against a fixed baseline
  • The publisher cannot say why the dynamic option was chosen beyond it being the provider's recommendation
  • A third-party floor provider sets the values and the publisher has not checked them against a floor they control
You may want to confirm that the dynamic-versus-fixed choice was made on evidence rather than by default. One approach is to hold a fixed-floor baseline on part of the inventory long enough to compare it against the dynamic floor on the rest.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements such as a homepage takeover slot or a sponsorship-adjacent unit often carry a price the publisher set on purpose to protect direct-sold value. You may want to keep a fixed-floor baseline on those units while running a dynamic floor elsewhere, so the comparison shows whether the dynamic model respects the premium or clears below it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can command higher prices than editorial pages because the audience is closer to a purchase. You may want to compare a dynamic floor against a fixed floor on the commercial pages specifically, rather than judging it on a blended average that the lower-value editorial inventory pulls down.

learn_more.source_title: Module - Price Floors for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for dynamic floors on the SSP side is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether the floors come from a fixed value the publisher set or from a dynamic model or provider, and look for any test result or before-and-after read that compares a dynamic floor against a fixed baseline.

prebid_config.label: Prebid wrapper configuration
steps: Inspect the Prebid Price Floors Module configuration and note whether floors are set as hard-coded ad unit values, a static package, or dynamic floors loaded from a provider endpoint.
If dynamic floors are in use, record which provider supplies them and whether any fixed-floor slice exists that could serve as a comparison baseline.
Ask the publisher whether any experiment or before-and-after comparison was run, and record what the result showed and when it was measured.

ssp_reporting.label: OpenX and wrapper reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory the floor targets, with revenue, impressions, fill rate, and average eCPM.
Compare a slice running a dynamic floor against a comparable slice on a fixed floor, if one exists, to see whether the dynamic floor raised revenue or cleared at a lower price.
Note whether the data supports the current floor choice or whether no fixed-floor slice exists to compare against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per slice of inventory, whether the floor is fixed or dynamic, which provider supplies a dynamic floor, and whether any comparison between a dynamic floor and a fixed baseline exists with a result on record. A setup where the dynamic-versus-fixed choice is deliberate and backed by a comparison passes. A setup where dynamic floors are applied across inventory with no fixed-floor comparison and no stated reason for the choice is a fail recorded with the inventory and provider affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor type for each slice of inventory and whether a dynamic-versus-fixed comparison exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs dynamic floors without a fixed-floor comparison on record, so the choice between dynamic and fixed floors has not been tested. A dynamic floor sets the price to maximize yield, which often helps, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium price point, and without a baseline the difference goes unmeasured. The case is stronger to check when a third-party provider supplies the dynamic values because the model's objective was set by an outside party. {{voice_recommend}} holding a fixed-floor baseline on a slice of comparable inventory, running the dynamic floor on the rest, and comparing revenue, fill rate, and average price across both before settling on a floor strategy. After the comparison, keep whichever floor the data supports for each slice of inventory.
